title: Pixel Movement Vectors
concept: Optical flow represents pixel motion using 2D vectors $(u, v)$ for each pixel, indicating horizontal and vertical displacement.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the main difference between apparent and real motion?

options.text: Apparent motion is just light change; real motion is physical change
Apparent motion only happens in black and white videos
Real motion does not create any pixel changes

exercises.explain: Yes! Optical flow tracks pixel changes, which can be caused by lighting shifts (apparent) or physical object movement (real).

cards.title: Mapping the map
cards.body: If you ask a friend how to get to the park, they don't just say 'move!' They give you a direction and a distance, like 'go 2 blocks North.' To describe pixel motion, computers do the exact same thing: they assign a small arrow (vector) to each moving pixel.

cards.title: Flow Vectors (u, v)
cards.body:
Optical flow represents motion at a pixel using a 2D vector $(u, v)$, where:
• $u$ = horizontal velocity (displacement along the X-axis)
• $v$ = vertical velocity (displacement along the Y-axis)
A positive $u$ means moving right; positive $v$ means moving down.

cards.title: A weather wind map
cards.body: Think of a weather map showing wind speeds. It is covered in little arrows pointing in the direction of the wind, with longer arrows representing faster wind. An optical flow map is a wind map for the pixels in your video!

cards.title: Calculating a vector
cards.body:
A pixel at coordinates $(10, 15)$ in Frame 1 moves to $(12, 14)$ in Frame 2.
• Horizontal change $u = 12 - 10 = +2$
• Vertical change $v = 14 - 15 = -1$
The movement vector for this pixel is $(+2, -1)$.

cards.title: Not a color image
cards.body: You might see colorful optical flow visualizations in textbooks. In these plots, color is just a trick! We map vector **direction** to hue (e.g., red for left, blue for right) and vector **magnitude** to brightness, so humans can read the vector math easily.

cards.title: Horizontal vs. Vertical Flow
cards.body: Comparing horizontal and vertical motion vector components:

left.text:
Measures X-axis displacement
Positive value: Moving right
Negative value: Moving left

right.text:
Measures Y-axis displacement
Positive value: Moving down
Negative value: Moving up

exercises.prompt: What does a vector value of u = -5 and v = 0 mean for a pixel?

options.text: The pixel moved 5 units to the left
The pixel moved 5 units to the right
The pixel did not move at all

exercises.explain: Correct! A negative u represents movement to the left along the horizontal X-axis, while v = 0 means no vertical movement.

exercises.prompt: True or false: An optical flow visualization uses color hue to represent the direction of a motion vector.
exercises.explain: True. Direction is typically color-coded (hue) and speed is represented by color intensity/brightness.

exercises.sentence: If a pixel moves straight up, its horizontal component 'u' is zero and its vertical component 'v' is ___.
exercises.explain: Since screen coordinates increase downwards, moving up results in a negative change in the Y axis (v < 0).

exercises.prompt: Which of these pairs represents a pixel motion vector?
exercises.explain: An optical flow vector is defined by its horizontal (u) and vertical (v) displacement values.

exercises.prompt: If a ball is moving diagonally down and to the right, what will be the signs of the u and v flow vector components?

options.text: Both u and v will be positive
u will be positive, v will be negative
u will be negative, v will be positive
Both u and v will be negative

exercises.explain: Exactly! Rightward movement means positive u, and downward movement means positive v in standard pixel coordinate systems. Next: what rules do we assume to solve these equations?
